title: Celiac Disease in Children
chief_complaint: Poor growth, chronic diarrhea, and abdominal distension for 1 year. She's fallen off her growth curve from 50th to 5th percentile. She's pale, tired, and irritable.

physical_exam.General: Thin, pale child. Muscle wasting in buttocks and thighs.
physical_exam.Abdomen: Distended (potbelly appearance). Diffuse mild tenderness. No organomegaly.
physical_exam.Growth: Weight <3rd percentile. Height 5th percentile. BMI <3rd percentile.

hidden_data.ttg_iga: Tissue Transglutaminase IgA >100 U/mL (normal <4) - markedly elevated
hidden_data.biopsy: Duodenal biopsy: Marsh 3b villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, intraepithelial lymphocytosis
hidden_data.bone_age: Delayed bone age (5 years vs chronological 8 years)

correct_diagnosis: Celiac Disease with Failure to Thrive, Iron Deficiency Anemia, and Delayed Bone Age
key_learning_points: Celiac disease = autoimmune enteropathy triggered by gluten in genetically susceptible children. Can present at any age after gluten introduction (typically 6-24 months)

Classic presentation in children: Failure to thrive, chronic diarrhea, abdominal distension, irritability, muscle wasting. Atypical: Isolated short stature, anemia, dental enamel defects, delayed puberty, arthritis
Diagnosis: tTG-IgA (screening) + EMA (confirmatory) + duodenal biopsy (gold standard). MUST be on gluten-containing diet. HLA-DQ2/DQ8 for negative serology but high suspicion
Treatment: STRICT LIFELONG GLUTEN-FREE DIET. Nutritional supplementation (iron, folate, B12, vitamin D, calcium). Monitor growth, tTG-IgA titers, and nutritional parameters
Complications of untreated celiac: Osteoporosis, infertility, lymphoma (EATL), other autoimmune diseases. Refractory celiac = no improvement after 12 months strict GFD
